sentences.text: Sure.
Gotfryd.
My name is Bernard Gotfryd, I was born in May of 1924, in Radom, Poland.
I had two other siblings and many, many relatives-- cousins, aunts and uncles, and grandparents, friends, associates, and God knows a huge extended family.
Yes.
No.
We did up to a point.
At some point-- [? the earliest ?] [INAUDIBLE] is my grandmother, my mother's mother, Elka, moved in with us when she got a little tired of living alone.
So she decided to live with us and-- until she left for Palestine, which was in 1936, I think.
She went to Palestine to join her-- she had two other daughters living in Palestine.
And she wanted to die in Palestine.
This was her aim, to die in Palestine, the Holy Land.
And I remember, it was a sad day when she left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I especially was, was attached to her.
I was the youngest of, of the three.
And I was her baby.
We had a three bedroom apartment, with some extra space.
It was kind of-- it was enough.
Until we got to be in our teens, then it got a little tight.
But we managed.
My father was a struggling merchant, furniture.
This is where most of his family were involved, in furniture.
It was Abraham Gotfryd and Sons, except that my father had decided to go on his own, which later turned out to be a mistake.
He should have stuck with his father.
Then chances are, he would've been better off.
But he decided to-- excuse me-- he was a very independent man.
And he wanted to prove that he could make it alone.
But being that the depression hit at the-- at the time, in the '30s, it was very difficult to make it.
And he struggled.
Well, at some point the business failed.
Then he had to give up the business.
And then he went to open a different business not far from the city, in a little-- in a community called [INAUDIBLE], which is something like a half an hour by train from Radom.
I was still in public school.
I was probably, at the time, in sixth grade, I think.
So I had to commute to school.
Because the school out there in the village was not very much up-to-date.
And so we decided that I'll have to commute and to continue at the same school I went to, which was a public school, except that it had very high standards at that time.
And for two years, until I finished seventh grade, I had to commute by train.
It was a very interesting time for me.
I, I love trains.
This is probably where I fell in love with trains.
Wherever I go, whatever country I visit, the first thing I do is take a ride on the train, even till now.
Nowadays, I still love trains.
No matter how bad they are, I love to listen to the sirens, the lo-- the locomotive, the whistle.
Of course, they are not as-- not the same whistles as they used to be in those days.
OK.
In the school I went to, we had seven Jewish boys in the class of '38, '40.
Yes.
Mickiewicza, named after a 19th c-- 19th century Polish poet, Adam Mickiewicz, who was quite prominent as a poet.
Oh yes, very much so.
This was very rampant, as a matter of fact.
But we learned how to live with it, although I-- we used to get our beatings regularly.
Still--
From other students, yes, from other students, for the simple reason that the Jewish kids were good.
We had to prove that we are-- we simply had to work hard.
And we had to be good, or else, what else was there left for you but to get a good education as possible?
And the average Polish kid simply wasn't motivated as much as the Jewish kid was.
You had it at home.
It was instilled in you, since you were-- since you started to walk.
You became aware of the fact that you are Jewish, that you are-- you simply have to achieve.
It was a very simple matter.
So you worked hard.
And the average Polish kid simply wasn't as motivated as the Jewish kid was.
So, if you were good-- if you were good, you had to pay a price.
It was simply envy.
And I remember I once turned in a paper.
And I got the best mark in the class.
And of course, it was announced.
And the teachers liked to announce these things.
And it was-- which was a mistake, which was my-- which was a, a problem.
During intermission, I took a terrible beating from three or four of them.
They ganged up on you.
And they simply told you-- oh, you're so smart, aren't you?
Well, let's see-- let's see how smart you are.
Boom.
You know?
Of course, I wound up with a bloody nose and bumped face and black and blue eyes.
I mean, I took a hell of a beating.
And I was not about to fight four kids.
So as soon as I got away, I ran to my father's shop, which was not far from the school down Main Street a few-- a couple of blocks.
I came into the store.
And my father was absolutely sho-- in shock.
He said, what happened to you?
He thought some-- something ran me over.
I said, well, I took a beating from kids.
He said, come with me.
This time, he said, she's not going to get away with it.
We had a principal, a woman, at the time.
And the intermission was still on.
It was towards the end of the intermission-- it was something like 10 or 15 minutes, I think.
He walked back with me to school.
And we eventually made it to her office.
And she was-- I remember, like now, she was sitting at her desk.
And as soon as we came in, she said, well, what is it now?
And my father said to her, look at him.
Take a look at my boy.
He said, it's not enough that they beat him up for no reason at all.
On top of it, he says, they call him dirty Jew.
And she turns around.
She looks at him-- well, isn't he?
So much about being a principal of a Polish public school.
At that point, my father was absolutely-- I remember, I looked at him.
He went pale.
And he said, is this what you call an education?
Is this what you call an educational institution?
And you are the principal?
Thank you, he said.
And we left.
And on the way down, he said, come, come to the pl-- to the store.
We'll wash your face, he said.
And I don't know what to tell you, he says.
But maybe you'll have to learn how to fight back.
But I said, but they ganged up on me.
He said, I know.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, this was not a daily occurrence, but these things did happen.
And there were other situations where I remember.
During-- middle of the winter, somebody threw a wet snowball right into my eye.
And my eye really swelled up tremendously.
And again, I ran to the store and showed my father.
And I remember my father saying, take a brick and throw it back at the kid who threw that snowball at you.
He said, do the same thing to him.
But I said, I just can't do that.
He said, you'll have to learn.
There's nothing we can do, he says.
We live in an anti-Semitic country.
They don't like us, he says.
And you are a target the way I, I am a target, he said.
So eventually, it sank in.
But I still could not fight back-- I mean, not under those terms.
I could not against such odds.
But eventually, there were situations where I did fight back when I had one individual.
And then I realized that this is the only option.
There's, there's nothing else.
You cannot re-educate them.
You cannot tell them-- you cannot make them love you.
And therefore, you simply have to deal with them on, on their own terms.
Occasionally, I did.
Of course, I got my licks, but I also realized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for us, it's the only thing left.
And so much about anti-Semitism-- and of course, again, we showed-- we saw there is also anti-Semitism during the Nazi occupation.
We-- there were many incidents daily where Polish anti-Semites would only applaud when the Germans would march down Jews or cut their beards off.
In the beginning, in the first days of occupation, some young SS troopers would walk down the street.
And whenever-- wherever they saw a Jew with a beard, they would take him and pa-- parade him in the middle of the street, take out huge scissors, and cut their beards.
And everybody would photograph the scene.
And the Polish anti-Semites would stand by and applaud these scenes.
It was a horrible, horrible sight.
It was a devastating feeling, to watch these things.
And then I realized that there is really so little one could do about it at that time.
Well, then it was too late, of course.
I mean, we were occupied.
The Nazis were-- but because they attracted a lot of those-- that element, that--
Well, to go back again to before the war broke out-- in your home, were you very religious?
What were Shabbos like?
We were not very religious, but traditionally so, I would say.
We-- my mother kept a kosher home.
And we went to shul on holidays.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, and it was a, I would say, a Jewish home in many respects.
We were not assimilated, maybe in some cultural sense, but not religiously.
We were an, an observant family, yes.
What languages did you speak in, in your home?
We spoke a lot of Yiddish, Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I, I remember my sister took German in school at some point.
And I picked up German from her.
Whatever-- I followed her lessons.
As a matter of fact, when the Nazis came in in '39, my German wasn't bad at all.
I was able to communicate.
And eventually, I learned French, I studied French.
During the war, I studied French.
And we had underground schools, gatherings at people's homes.
And we had teachers who, who would come to somebody's house in the ghetto.
Before the ghetto yet, 1940. 1939, '40, '41.
The ghetto was established in 1941, in April.
So until then, we would meet in people's homes and study all the different subjects, languages, whoever, whatever was offered.
And this is how I learned my French at the time.
I studied French.
'Theft of a Table,' yes, yes.
Yeah, I wrote it very vividly, sure.
I think I was very [? authentic. ?] This was in the country where we used to go out to, every summer when we were kids.
Do you want me to recall the incident?
Oh.
And there was a veranda attached to the, to the cottage where we lived.
This was a village called [?
Antonowka ?] about one hour's or less drive from the city-- lovely country, very unspoiled, all the farmland.
And we rented this cottage from the farmers, Mr.
Joseph.
And he built a veranda for us, like a porch, because we didn't have much room in the cottage.
It was one huge room.
And then we were able to use the kitchen with his wife-- it was their kitchen-- jointly.
And we brought with us a table, a hardwood table, with a drawer.
And we placed the table on the, the veranda.
That table was really everything.
It was the most important piece of furniture.
I remember it had a lower shelf.
And on that lower shelf, my grandmother kept a lot of different things, households things.
And in the drawer, I kept my, my items, whatever-- wooden soldiers, metal little objects I had.
I even had the sling that I made.
I used to practice-- I used to practice on some-- whatever-- little animals I could find.
Of course, I I was a bad shot, anyway.
Anyway-- and one night, sure enough, the table was stolen.
My grandmother was with us at the time-- Grandmother Elka.
And she, one morning-- she was the first to get up in the morning.
And the first thing, of course, she would do, is go out to the veranda, start putting around, getting ready for breakfast.
And suddenly, I-- we hear her say, where is the table?
What happened to the table?
And of course, slowly and surely, we all got up to see that there's no table in the veranda, gone.
Couldn't understand whatever happened to the table!
It was a rather large, heavy table-- hardwood table.
I mean, it wouldn't walk away all by itself.
So we realized that somebody must have taken it.
They stole the table.
And they shouldn't have cause-- within a few minutes, the word spread.
Mr.
Joseph came around, our landlord at the farm.
And we told him what happened.
And he got on a horse, because there was-- there was no precinct in this village.
He had to go to the next larger village.
He got on, on the horse.
And he rode away to notify the police.
There were no telephones.
And this is like 18th century, you know, really, in our terms.
And sure enough, oh, a good hour later, the policeman showed up on a bicycle with his rifle slung across his chest and back.
And slowly and surely, he got to work.
He asked some questions, and how large was the table?
And when was it here we saw it the last time.
And my grandmother officiated.
And she was taking-- she was answering all the questions.
And then he started out.
And this was-- the ground was still wet from-- it must  have rained at night.
And apparently, he found tracks of those wooden legs of the table on the ground.
And I remember, he found a straw.
He measured the width of the track with the straw.
And then he broke it, And this is how he kept on walking.
And at that time, that morning my brother's friend was visiting, visiting with us.
And for some reason, we were all fascinated by it, to see a policeman with a, with a gun on his-- over his shoulder, doing all these strange things, you know-- measuring tracks.
And we followed him.
And I went with them.
I was the, the youngest of the lot.
And my sister was there, too-- the four of us.
And it was just an incredible scene to see these four young kids follow the policemen.
And at some point, he had to also drag the bicycle along with him, the policeman.
At some point, we were out in the middle of the field-- it was a potato field along a narrow path.
And he turns around.
And he says to my brother's friend, whose nickname was [?
Tzytzek. ?] And he said to him, now, you take care of my bike.
And he was, oh, sure, sure, of course he will.
And I remember as soon as he got the bike, he rang, he rang the little bell that he had attached to it.
And he got very annoyed.
The policeman turned around and sort of looked at him.
He said, don't do that.
At that point, some birds took off.
They were nesting, probably, somewhere in the, in the area.
And the commotion that took place because of that little ring-- it was so nice and quiet.
I mean, for miles and miles, you couldn't see a thing move, really.
And we just kept following him.
And eventually, we made it over-- we had to cross a railroad-- railroad tracks.
And on the other side, there was a halfway burned out house-- farmhouse and a barn.
And the policeman said to us, now, you all stay here.
Don't make a move.
Don't talk, and be quiet, and all that.
And he went into the barn.
And sure enough, this is where he found the table.
As it turned out, this couple of farmers lost everything they had in this fire that they had in this house.
The whole roof was gone.
And probably the whole interior was burnt down.
All they had left were the walls-- left of the house and the little barn.
And they decide-- they needed a table.
And this was why they decided-- it must have been easy, easy prey to just walk in and take it away, right?
So they decided to take this table, which they did.
And eventually, the policeman came out.

And I remember the farmer's wife-- a woman came out, kind of very tall and skinny, crossing herself and begging him to let her go, that she, that she didn't-- how did she say-- that it was God's will.
And it was all God's will, that they really are-- that they really are decent people.
We are very decent people, she kept on saying, crossing herself.
It was all God's will.
And the policeman insisted that fin-- the table was already taken apart.
And finally, he-- the poor husband of hers, the farmer, had to carry the top of the table on his back, which was quite a heavy load.
And some of us carried a leg each.
It was very funny.
Anyway, we-- the way we finally, we got-- we came back with the table back to the cottage.
And of course, there was my grandmother.
And unbelievable-- I mean, she was absolutely overwhelmed with this whole thing.
Look, you found it!
You found it!
Where was it?
Who took it?
And sure enough, she got the whole story after a minute.
And she looked at those two people, that farmer with his wife.
And I remember her saying-- she stood there, wringing her hands, saying, oh, poor people.
[LAUGHS] And of-- they must be hungry.
And the first thing she did, she runs into the cottage and brought out bread and milk.
Whatever she had in the house, she brought it out.
And she said to the wife, just sit down and have something.
You must be hungry, And they were hungry.
And we couldn't get over it-- I mean, the policeman finds crooks who stole the table, brings them back.
And my grandmother feeds them.
And she feels sorry for them.
And eventually, it was the most incredible scene.
We just couldn't get over with the fact how she treated-- to ask-- they were crooks, you know?
The good guys and the bad guys-- these were the bad guys.
They steal, right?
And here comes this grandmother of ours and feeds them.
And it was absolutely unbelievable.
And I do know one thing, that she didn't want the policeman to arrest them.
She said that no, she's not going to press any charges.
Because he asked her, are you going to press charges?
She says, no, they were-- how did she say-- they were punished enough.
They lost everything.
They have nothing left.
They were punished enough.
Why to punish them more-- something like that.
The policeman looked at her.
And he sort of was wondering, is she all right?
What is she talking about?
Here I go out of my way, finding the table and finding the people who steal the table, and she's protecting them-- ridiculous.
Sure enough, the-- it ended in a very weird fashion, too.
He had to take them for some formality, to the precinct.
And my grandmother told them, look, why don't you come back here before we leave?
This was towards the end of the summer.
We were leaving in a couple more weeks or so-- we were going back to the city.
And she said, I'll give you the table.
You'll-- you can have the table.
You come back whenever will-- before we leave.
And she told them exactly when we were going to be leaving.
And sure enough, a day or two before we were leaving back to the city, they showed up with a little hand wagon to get the table.
And she gave them the table.
Well, the table was no more in the shape that it used to be.
Because they really-- they damaged it by taking it apart.
This table should have never been taken apart.
But it was too heavy-- it was too big to hide in one piece.
So they figured they'll take it apart.
It'll be easier to hide in the barn, which for some reason didn't work, either.
And in that wagon, they had a chicken and wild flowers.
And I remember the woman grabbed the chicken.
And she brought it.
The chicken had-- a live chicken-- she tied the feet.
The chicken's feet were tied with a rag.
And she brought it to my grandmother.
And she says, here.
This is for you.
This is our gift.
And the man brought a huge bouquet of wildflowers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandmother was all, well, oh, isn't that nice of you?
Thank you so much.
[LAUGHS] And she-- she was-- she really-- I, I mean, I, I, I remember we were wondering, what is this?
I mean, something is wrong here, you know?
Where is justice?
What is this?
What does it all mean, right?
And this was it.
We were-- a day or two later, we went back to the city.
Mr.
Joseph drove us back in his big wagon-- horse and wagon.
I was sitting next to her.
And she had this chicken in her lap, ca-- caressing the chicken's head.
And at some point, she says to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what am I going to tell your mother?
[LAUGHS] What am I going to tell your mother, what, what happened to the table?
And she started laughing hysterically.
And sure enough-- I mean, we had plenty of tables, except that-- I don't remember precisely what she told my parents happened to the table.
But I knew that-- I, I realized that they eventually found out that she gave the table away, that the table simply got to be too much.
It was too big.
It was too old.
It was falling apart.
And it didn't pay anymore to schlep it back and forth.
So why not to give it away?
We'll take a smaller table next time along.
But of course, mother knew about-- we-- I think my sister told mother exactly what happened one evening.
And Mother said, that's my mother.

[LAUGHS] That's a, that's a, that's a story I'll, I'll remember it forever, probably, just to be there and to list-- to, just to watch her expressions when all this was happening, how she-- how sorry she felt for those two farmers, those poor people-- amazing.
Gutman, yes--
Well, he warned father about the fact that there is no future for Jews in Poland.
It was as simple as that.
It was-- it became a very general issue.
I mean, everybody knew about it.
He was not the only one who was aware of it, except that he was more aware than a lot of other people.
He was a very interesting man.
I probably-- he, of all the people who would come into the house, stands out most in my mind, the most fascinating character.
As a child-- well, seven, eight-- I used to listen.
I used to love to listen to his stories.
And he would-- he would just have some amazingly interesting stories to tell.
There was always a story to learn something from life.
There was always something that was absolutely fascinating, that was interesting that-- it made a lot of sense, you know?
And mainly, he spoke about existence, and culture, and future, and the future of the Jews as a people.
And there is no future in this country-- that we must do everything possible to simply vacate this land, because it will never be good for us.
And we must think in terms of our own-- a roof over our own, over our own head.
And he, and he spoke a great deal about Israel-- that is Palestine at that time-- especially.
And he was very supportive of these-- of illegal immigration, the Halutzim projects.
Whether you were aware of at that time, young people would train in countries in Europe, getting ready for Palestine-- to emigrate illegally to Palestine.
So they would train on farms.
They would set-- set up a collective farm for the Halutzim pioneers.
We are talking about boys and girls in their late teens or mid teens.
They would spend a year or two on the collectives, get ready-- get acquainted with farming [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mainly.
And eventually, they would emigrate.
These were the pioneers.

These were the people-- the sort of people who, when they arrived to Palestine in their, early 30s, late 20s, these were the people who really, who dried the swamps, who did all the dirty work, who eventually became the leaders of-- when Palestine became a state, when, when Palestine became Israel.
Well.
